D1.name: Disenfranchised (Low-Income, Minority, Rural, Disabled, Elderly)
D1.primary_stance: Support humane policies that protect vulnerable populations, oppose enforcement that separates families or criminalizes survival
policy_preferences: End to detention of vulnerable populations

D2.name: Privileged (High-Income, Majority, Urban)
D2.primary_stance: Prioritize legal immigration pathways, support enforcement of existing laws, concerned about system integrity and resource allocation

D3.name: Experts (Economists, Political Scientists, Sociologists, Immigration Lawyers)
D3.primary_stance: Data-driven approach to policy, emphasize evidence over ideology, prioritize systemic solutions over political expediency

D4.name: Affected Stakeholders (Farmers, Employers, Service Workers, Communities)
D4.primary_stance: Pragmatic approach balancing economic needs with community concerns, focus on implementation realities

D5.name: Ideological (Progressive, Conservative, Libertarian, etc.)
D5.primary_stance: Varies by ideology: Progressives prioritize human rights, Conservatives prioritize order, Libertarians prioritize freedom
policy_preferences: Progressive: Universal human rights, open borders (or managed migration)
Conservative: Law and order, national sovereignty, cultural preservation
Libertarian: Freedom of movement, limited government, free markets

D6.name: Geographic (Urban, Suburban, Rural, Coastal, Inland, Border States)
D6.primary_stance: Policies should account for geographic variation in needs, resources, and priorities

D7.name: Age Demographics (Gen Z, Millennials, Gen X, Boomers, Silent)
D7.primary_stance: Different generations have distinct perspectives shaped by economic conditions, technology, and social context
policy_preferences: Gen Z/Millennials: More open, climate-focused, social justice
Gen X/Boomers: Balanced, economic pragmatism, stability

D8.name: Professional Sectors (Healthcare, Education, Tech, Manufacturing, etc.)
D8.primary_stance: Policies should support sector-specific workforce needs while maintaining professional standards and ethics

D9.name: Cultural/Ethnic Groups (Hispanic, Asian, Black, White, Indigenous)
D9.primary_stance: Concerned with how policies affect cultural groups, particularly regarding discrimination and representation

D10.name: Religious Groups (Christian, Muslim, Jewish, Secular, etc.)
D10.primary_stance: Faith-based perspectives emphasize compassion and hospitality, while secular perspectives emphasize universal rights

D11.name: Environmental Advocates
D11.primary_stance: Policies should consider environmental impact, resource sustainability, and climate-driven migration

D12.name: Economic Traditionalists
D12.primary_stance: Policies should promote economic stability, fiscal responsibility, and long-term economic health

D1.argument_text: Over-emphasis on humanitarian considerations without addressing systemic root causes may perpetuate dependency
D1.potential_bias: Focus on immediate relief over long-term solutions

D1.argument_text: Pathways to citizenship without enforcement may undermine rule of law and create perception of unfairness to law-abiding immigrants
D1.potential_bias: Potential for absolutist view of legal compliance

D2.argument_text: Overemphasis on enforcement can create humanitarian crises and damage U.S. moral standing internationally
Focus on taxpayer burden may overlook economic contributions of immigrants, including undocumented workers

D3.argument_text: Over-reliance on quantitative metrics may overlook qualitative humanitarian and ethical dimensions of immigration
Expert-driven policies may lack democratic legitimacy and fail to account for public sentiment and values

D4.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D5.argument_text: Ideological rigidity can prevent pragmatic solutions and lead to policy failure in complex real-world contexts
Polarized ideological positions may undermine social cohesion and make compromise impossible

D6.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D7.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D8.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D9.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D10.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

D11.argument_text: Over-emphasis on environmental impacts may忽视 other priorities and disproportionately affect developing nations
Climate migration is complex and cannot be addressed through immigration policy alone

D12.argument_text: This perspective may overlook trade-offs between competing values
Implementation challenges may undermine theoretical benefits

Immigrants contribute more in taxes than.claim: Immigrants contribute more in taxes than they receive in benefits

Undocumented immigrants commit crimes at.claim: Undocumented immigrants commit crimes at similar rates to citizens

Immigration has positive long-term econo.claim: Immigration has positive long-term economic impact

economics.labor_markets: Mixed impacts on different wage groups
economics.fiscal_impact: Variable fiscal effects across levels of government
economics.economic_growth: Potential for positive long-term growth impact

sociology.network_effects: Immigrant networks have significant economic benefits

psychology.emotional_factors: Strong emotional responses to immigration issues

key_insights: Political feasibility is critical for implementation
Economic benefits require complementary social policies
Social integration determines long-term success
Public perception must be managed through transparent communication
recommendations: Combine economic analysis with social impact assessment
Consider political feasibility and implementation capacity
Address both material and social dimensions of integration

llm_call_logs.input_context: Analyzing immigration policies across 12 societal perspectives
reasoning_steps: Calculate support levels and concerns for each perspective
Identify key concerns and benefits per policy

llm_call_logs.input_context: Comparing all perspective pairs for agreements and contradictions
reasoning_steps: Generate comparison matrix for all perspective pairs
Identify common ground between opposing views
Determine policy alignment for each pair

llm_call_logs.output: Analysis reveals tension between humanitarian concerns and rule of law/秩序 concerns
Experts emphasize evidence, ideological perspectives emphasize values
Geographic focuses on location, cultural on identity
Generated counter-arguments and verified claims across all perspectives

reasoning_steps: Check each policy for anti-pattern indicators
llm_call_logs.output: Detected anti-patterns in current policy framework
